notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Great question, Priya — and the answer is yes, a backdoor Roth IRA is worth considering, but it typically makes sense after you've fully captured several higher-impact moves first. Let me walk you through the priority stack.
Since tax and retirement strategies involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Priority order for 2026
Step 1 — Capture your full employer 401(k) match
Both your Salesforce and UCHealth 401(k) plans show up in your connected accounts. Before anything else, make sure your contributions to each plan are at least high enough to capture the full employer match. That's an immediate 50–100% return on those dollars — nothing else in the priority stack beats it.
Step 2 — Max your HSA (if you're on a high-deductible health plan)
Your HealthEquity HSA account is connected. If you're enrolled in an HDHP, the HSA is arguably the most powerful account available — contributions reduce taxable income now, growth is tax-free, and qualified medical withdrawals are also tax-free. The 2026 limits are $4,400 for self-only coverage or $8,750 for family coverage, plus a $1,000 catch-up if you're 55 or older.
Step 3 — Max your 401(k) employee deferrals
The 2026 employee deferral limit is $24,500 (with an $8,000 catch-up if you're 50–59 or 64+, or $11,250 if you're 60–63). Doing this across both your Salesforce and UCHealth plans (keeping in mind the $24,500 total across all employers) should come before a backdoor Roth.
Step 4 — Check if a direct Roth IRA is still available to you
If your MAGI is below $153,000 (single) or $242,000 (married filing jointly), you can contribute directly to a Roth IRA — up to $7,500 in 2026, or $8,600 if you're 50 or older. A direct contribution is simpler than a backdoor Roth because there's no pro-rata complexity.
When the backdoor Roth makes sense
If your income exceeds those Roth phase-out thresholds, the backdoor Roth becomes the right next move. The mechanics are straightforward: make a nondeductible traditional IRA contribution (up to $7,500), then convert it to a Roth IRA shortly after, and file Form 8606 to document your basis.
The key watch-out is the pro-rata rule — if you have existing pre-tax IRA balances (like a rollover IRA), the IRS blends all your traditional, SEP, and SIMPLE IRA balances together when calculating the taxable portion of the conversion. A large pre-tax IRA balance can make a backdoor Roth surprisingly costly. One common solution is rolling those pre-tax IRA funds into your 401(k) before year-end, if your plan allows it.
Step 5 — Mega backdoor Roth (if your 401(k) plan allows it)
If your Salesforce or UCHealth plan permits after-tax contributions and either in-plan Roth conversions or in-service distributions, you could potentially move significantly more into Roth space — the total 401(k) limit in 2026 is $72,000, well above the $24,500 deferral limit. This step often has a bigger impact than the standard $7,500 backdoor Roth IRA, so it's worth checking your plan documents.
The bottom line
The backdoor Roth is a genuinely useful tool — but it's most valuable after you've captured your employer match, maxed your HSA, and maximized your 401(k) deferrals. For a $7,500 annual contribution, the complexity is manageable, but the pro-rata rule is the detail that trips most people up.
I don't have your income or tax filing details on file, which would help me give you a more tailored picture — would you like to share those so we can figure out exactly where you stand in this priority stack?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
